name: Muslim Advocates
mission: TO PROMOTE EQUALITY, LIBERTY AND JUSTICE FOR ALL, BY PROVIDING LEADERSHIP THROUGH LEGAL ADVOCACY, POLICY ENGAGEMENT AND CIVIC EDUCATION AND BY SERVING A...
theoryOfChange: Muslim Advocates outlines its strategic framework in its Annual Report, detailing how it measures impact against three core pillars: building community power, dismantling discriminatory systems, and demanding shared wellbeing. These principles guide their work to create transformative change through high-impact litigation, policy engagement, public advocacy, narrative-shifting communications, and training. The organization measures success for 'Building Community Power' by assessing communities' ability to develop, organize, and act together to shift narratives, influence decision-makers, and achieve transformative change for justice.

dimension_explanations.alignment: With an alignment score of 29/50, the charity addresses urgent civil rights causes but may have a lower fit for donors seeking high-efficiency direct aid [1][8]. Its status as Zakat-eligible provides a specific religious alignment for Muslim donors, supported by its focus on legal justice and policy reform [8].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization focuses on capacity building through legal awards and settlements, though its impact score of 26/50 reflects a moderate program expense ratio of 54.9% [2][5]. While it successfully holds 7.8 months of working capital, the directness of its impact is centered on long-term advocacy rather than direct service delivery [1][6].

baseline_narrative.summary: Based in Washington, D.C., the organization provides legal representation and litigation services to protect the rights of all Americans, with a specific focus on the Muslim community [1][2]. In the most recent fiscal period, the charity reported a total revenue of $1,891,645 to support its mission of civic education and policy advocacy [2][3].

strengths.detail: The organization maintains <cite id="5">7.8 months of working capital</cite>, ensuring stability for long-term legal battles.
Successfully secured <cite id="7">$90,628 in legal awards and settlements</cite> through its litigation efforts.
The organization is <cite id="11">Zakat-eligible</cite>, serving categories such as fisabilillah to protect the community's civil rights.

strengths_deep_dive: Muslim Advocates distinguishes itself through its <cite id="1">specialized legal representation</cite> and its role in <cite id="2">policy engagement</cite>. By maintaining <cite id="12">100% independent board members</cite>, the organization ensures a high level of governance oversight. Their ability to sustain operations despite a <cite id="4">-62.5% revenue CAGR</cite> highlights a disciplined approach to <cite id="5">working capital management</cite>, which currently stands at 7.8 months.
rich_narrative.summary: Muslim Advocates is a national civil rights organization that provides <cite id="1">legal representation and litigation services</cite> to protect the rights of the Muslim community and all Americans. Operating from Washington, D.C., the organization focuses on <cite id="2">policy engagement and civic education</cite> to combat discrimination. In the 2023 fiscal year, the organization reported <cite id="3">total revenue of $992,892</cite>, a significant decrease from previous years as evidenced by a <cite id="4">3-year revenue CAGR of -62.5%</cite>. Despite these financial shifts, the charity maintains a <cite id="5">solid liquidity position with 7.8 months of working capital</cite>, providing a buffer for its advocacy operations. The organization's work includes <cite id="6">high-level legal advocacy and settlements</cite>, specifically reporting <cite id="7">$90,628 in legal awards and settlements</cite> in recent filings. Their programmatic reach extends to <cite id="8">Muslim charities accreditation</cite> and providing 'Know Your Rights' materials to underserved populations. However, financial efficiency remains a point of observation, as the charity currently operates with a <cite id="9">program expense ratio of 54.9%</cite>, which is notably lower than the <cite id="10">peer program ratio median of 77.5%</cite> for advocacy organizations.
